phrases.cultural_note: Always open with 'Disculpe' or 'Buenos días' — Spaniards value the courtesy of a proper greeting before any request.

responses.english: Yes, how can I help you?
responses.phonetic: SEE, ehn keh leh PWEH-doh ah-yoo-DAR

phrases.pronunciation: NOH ehn-tee-EHN-doh. PWEH-deh reh-peh-TEER MÁS deh-spah-THYOH
phrases.cultural_note: Spaniards often speak quickly — asking to slow down is entirely normal and will be met with patience.
In Spain say 'los servicios' or 'el baño'. Cafés and bars are usually happy to let customers use the facilities.

responses.english: At the back on the right.

phrases.cultural_note: These two phrases open every door. Spaniards are warm and respond well to genuine courtesy.
Spaniards are genuinely helpful with directions. If they don't know, they'll often ask someone nearby on your behalf.

responses.phonetic: TOH-doh REHK-toh ee LWEH-goh ah lah ees-KYEHR-dah
responses.local: Todo recto y luego a la izquierda.

responses.phonetic: ehs-TÁ ah THEEN-koh mee-NOO-tohs ah PYEH
responses.local: Está a cinco minutos a pie.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for help in an emergency
phrases.cultural_note: Emergency number in Spain: 112. Police: 091. Medical: 061.

phrases.intent_en: Ask someone to speak more slowly
phrases.cultural_note: Perfectly natural to ask. Spaniards tend to speak fast and will happily slow down when asked.

phrases.text_local: No hablo español, solo un poco.
phrases.pronunciation: NOH AH-bloh ehs-pah-NYOL, SOH-loh oon POH-koh
phrases.cultural_note: Even a little Spanish goes a long way in Spain. Locals appreciate any effort and will often switch to English or use gestures to help.

phrases.pronunciation: BWEH-nohs DEE-ahs / BWEH-nahs TAR-dehs / BWEH-nahs NOH-chehs
phrases.cultural_note: Use 'Buenos días' until noon, 'Buenas tardes' until dusk, 'Buenas noches' in the evening. 'Buenas' alone works informally any time of day.

phrases.pronunciation: ah-DYOHS. keh TEHN-gah oon BWEHN DEE-ah
phrases.cultural_note: 'Adiós' is standard. 'Hasta luego' (see you later) is warmer. Always say goodbye when leaving a shop — it's good manners in Spain.
'Perdona' (informal) or 'Perdone' (formal) to get attention or apologise. 'Disculpe' is more formal. Both are used constantly in daily Spanish life.
Point at the item if you're unsure of the name. Most vendors are happy to show you the price on a screen or write it down.
All-purpose location question. Show the name on your phone if you can't pronounce it — Spaniards are always happy to help.
'Claro' (of course) and '¡Por supuesto!' (absolutely!) are strong affirmatives. 'Quizás' or 'A lo mejor' are the polite maybes.
Simple and clear. Add 'lo siento' (sorry) to soften. Spaniards who don't know the answer will often ask a colleague on your behalf.

phrases.intent_en: Ask someone to write it down
phrases.cultural_note: Invaluable for names, addresses, and prices. People will happily write it down or show you on their phone.

responses.english: I don't have paper, but I'll show you on my phone.
responses.phonetic: NOH TEHN-goh pah-PEHL, PEH-roh seh loh MWEHS-troh ehn ehl MOH-beel
responses.local: No tengo papel, pero se lo muestro en el móvil.

phrases.text_local: Buenas noches, ¿tienen una mesa para dos?
phrases.pronunciation: BWEH-nahs NOH-chehs, TYEH-nehn OO-nah MEH-sah PAH-rah dos
phrases.cultural_note: Always greet with 'Buenas noches' (evening) or 'Buenas tardes' (afternoon). Dinner in Spain starts late — restaurants often don't fill up until 9 or 10pm.
'La carta' is the full menu. 'El menú del día' is the fixed-price lunch (starter + main + dessert + drink) — usually excellent value and typically available 1–4pm.

responses.english: Shall I explain the set menu?
responses.phonetic: leh eks-PLEE-koh ehl meh-NOO dehl DEE-ah

phrases.intent_en: Ask for the dish of the day
phrases.pronunciation: KWAHL ehs ehl PLAH-toh dehl DEE-ah
phrases.cultural_note: The 'plato del día' rotates daily and uses fresh seasonal produce. It's often the best and freshest option on the menu.

responses.phonetic: ehl pehs-KAH-doh dehl DEE-ah ehs mehr-LOO-thah
responses.local: El pescado del día es merluza.

phrases.text_local: Soy alérgico a los frutos secos.
phrases.pronunciation: SOY ah-LEHR-hee-koh ah lohs FROO-tohs SEH-kohs
phrases.cultural_note: Swap 'frutos secos' (nuts) for your allergen: gluten, lácteos (dairy), huevos (eggs), mariscos (shellfish). Spanish kitchens take allergies seriously.

responses.english: OK, I'll mention it to the kitchen.
responses.phonetic: deh ah-KWEHR-doh, loh koh-MEHN-toh ehn koh-THEE-nah
responses.local: De acuerdo, lo comento en cocina.

phrases.text_local: Una jarra de agua del grifo, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: OO-nah HA-rrah deh AH-gwah dehl GREE-foh, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: Tap water is safe and free in Spain. Some restaurants will push bottled water — you're perfectly entitled to ask for 'agua del grifo'. In some regions it tastes better than others.
The waiter won't bring the bill unless asked — this is normal. Tips are not obligatory in Spain but rounding up or leaving 5–10% for good service is appreciated.

responses.english: Are you paying together or separately?

phrases.pronunciation: SOY beh-heh-tah-RYAH-noh. TYEH-nehn ohp-THYOH-nehs seen KAR-neh
phrases.cultural_note: Spain is increasingly vegetarian-friendly in cities. Traditional dishes to look for: gazpacho, pisto, patatas bravas, tortilla española. Rural areas have fewer options.

responses.english: Yes, we have several vegetarian dishes.

phrases.intent_en: Say you're still looking at the menu
phrases.text_local: Todavía estamos mirando la carta, gracias.
phrases.pronunciation: toh-dah-BEE-ah ehs-TAH-mohs mee-RAHN-doh lah KAR-tah, GRAH-thyahs
phrases.cultural_note: Spanish waiters are attentive — this phrase gives you space without being rude.
Catching a waiter's eye or a gentle 'Perdona' (excuse me) works well in Spanish restaurants.
Spanish waiters are proud of their food and will give genuine, enthusiastic recommendations — especially for local or seasonal specialities.

responses.english: I recommend the suckling pig, it's our speciality.
responses.phonetic: leh reh-koh-MYEHN-doh ehl koh-chee-NEE-yoh, ehs NWEHS-trah ehs-peh-thyah-lee-DAHD
responses.local: Le recomiendo el cochinillo, es nuestra especialidad.

responses.english: The dish of the day is very good today.
responses.phonetic: ehl PLAH-toh dehl DEE-ah ehs-TÁ mwee BWEH-noh OY
responses.local: El plato del día está muy bueno hoy.

phrases.cultural_note: Spanish menus often use regional names. Don't hesitate to ask — waiters enjoy explaining their dishes.

responses.english: It's a meat stew with seasonal vegetables.
responses.phonetic: ehs oon GHEE-soh deh KAR-neh kohn behr-DOO-rahs deh tehm-poh-RAH-dah
responses.local: Es un guiso de carne con verduras de temporada.

responses.phonetic: ehs OO-nah ehs-peh-thyah-lee-DAHD deh lah reh-HYOHN
responses.local: Es una especialidad de la región.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if a dish is spicy
phrases.cultural_note: Most Spanish cuisine is not hot-spicy. Exceptions: some Canarian dishes with mojo picón, patatas bravas sauce varies by bar. 'Picante' in Spain means chilli-hot.

responses.english: A little, but not too much.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for a dish without an ingredient
phrases.cultural_note: Swap with: cebolla (onion/theh-BOH-yah), ajo (garlic/AH-hoh), queso (cheese/KEH-soh), nata (cream/NAH-tah), sal (salt/SAHL).
Splitting bills is common in Spain among friends. Ask before the check arrives to make it easier for the server.

phrases.intent_en: Ask to split the bill evenly
phrases.cultural_note: The waiter will do the maths. You can also say 'hacemos a escote' — the informal Spanish term for splitting equally.

responses.phonetic: deh ah-KWEHR-doh, sohn BEYN-teh EH-oo-rohs KAH-dah OO-noh
responses.local: De acuerdo, son veinte euros cada uno.

phrases.text_local: Todo junto en una sola cuenta.
phrases.pronunciation: TOH-doh HOON-toh ehn OO-nah SOH-lah KWEHN-tah
phrases.cultural_note: Say this before anyone else tries to pay — it avoids confusion.

responses.english: Very good, I'll bring the bill now.
responses.phonetic: mwee BYEHN, ah-OH-rah leh TRAH-ee-goh lah KWEHN-tah
responses.local: Muy bien, ahora le traigo la cuenta.

phrases.text_local: ¿Tienen un tupper para llevar lo que sobra?
phrases.pronunciation: TYEH-nehn oon TOO-pehr PAH-rah yeh-BAR loh keh SOH-brah
phrases.cultural_note: Doggy bags are becoming more common in Spain. 'Para llevar' (to take away) is the phrase to know. Not all restaurants have containers — worth asking.
Terrace dining is a Spanish institution — locals spend hours at outdoor tables. In summer, ask for shade ('a la sombra'). Terraces often have separate menus or small surcharges.

responses.english: The terrace is full, would you prefer inside?
responses.phonetic: lah teh-RAH-thah ehs-TÁ YEH-nah, preh-FYEH-rehn DEHN-troh

phrases.cultural_note: Indoor seating is quieter and cooler in summer. In winter, Spanish terraces are often heated with gas heaters — specify if you want to be inside.

responses.english: We have a free table at the back.
responses.phonetic: teh-NEH-mohs OO-nah MEH-sah LEE-breh ahl FOHN-doh
responses.local: Tenemos una mesa libre al fondo.

phrases.intent_en: Say you have no reservation and ask about space
phrases.text_local: No tenemos reserva, ¿tienen alguna mesa libre?
phrases.pronunciation: NOH teh-NEH-mohs reh-SEHR-bah, TYEH-nehn ahl-GOO-nah MEH-sah LEE-breh
phrases.cultural_note: Without a reservation, try arriving at 9pm sharp or on a weekday. Popular restaurants in Madrid and Barcelona fill up fast on weekends.

responses.english: Yes, I'll seat you right now.
responses.phonetic: SEE, ah-OH-rah MEES-moh lehs POHN-goh ehn OO-nah
responses.local: Sí, ahora mismo les pongo en una.

responses.phonetic: loh see-EHN-toh, ehs-TAH-mohs kohm-PLEH-tohs EHS-tah NOH-cheh
responses.local: Lo siento, estamos completos esta noche.

phrases.cultural_note: Bread is complimentary at most Spanish restaurants. Don't hesitate to ask for more — it's expected.

phrases.intent_en: Say this isn't what you ordered
phrases.text_local: Esto no es lo que pedí.
phrases.pronunciation: EHS-toh NOH ehs loh keh peh-DEE
phrases.cultural_note: Say this calmly and point to the menu. Spanish staff will correct mistakes promptly and apologetically.

responses.english: Sorry, I'll correct it right now.

phrases.cultural_note: 'Una caña' is a small draft beer — the classic Spanish bar order. For a larger glass ask for 'un tercio'. In the Basque Country ask for 'un zurito'.

phrases.text_local: Una copa de vino tinto, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: OO-nah KOH-pah deh BEE-noh TEEN-toh, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: Swap 'tinto' (red) for 'blanco' (white) or 'rosado' (rosé). Spain produces excellent regional wines — ask about local options.

responses.phonetic: deh lah RYOH-hah oh deh lah KAH-sah
responses.local: ¿De la Rioja o de la casa?

phrases.intent_en: Ask what beers they have on tap
phrases.cultural_note: Most Spanish bars have one or two draft options. The barman may point to the taps. Mahou and Estrella are the most common national brands.

responses.english: We only have Mahou on draft.

phrases.cultural_note: Traditional Spanish bars focus on beer and wine. Look for 'bar de copas' or 'coctelería' for mixed drinks. Gin-tonic is enormously popular in Spain.

responses.english: Yes, what can I get you?
We only do gin-tonic and spirits with mixer.

phrases.cultural_note: In Spanish bars you often pay when you leave, not per drink. Tipping is not expected but leaving small change or rounding up is appreciated.

responses.phonetic: PAH-gah ehn eh-fehk-TEE-boh oh kohn tar-HEH-tah

phrases.intent_en: Order another round of the same
phrases.text_local: Lo mismo para todos, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: loh MEES-moh PAH-rah TOH-dohs, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: The classic way to order a round in Spain. You can also say 'Otra ronda' (another round) or just 'Lo mismo' for your own drink.
Running a tab is common at bars with table service. At a crowded bar counter, paying per drink is more usual — always ask first.

responses.english: We prefer to charge per drink.

phrases.cultural_note: Spanish bars keep late hours — many stay open until 2–3am. Nightclubs open at midnight and run until 6am. Licensing hours vary by region.
Common non-alcoholic options in Spanish bars: agua con gas, zumo (juice), Kas naranja (fizzy orange), mosto (grape juice), horchata (tiger nut drink, Valencia).

responses.english: We have juices, Kas, sparkling water…
responses.phonetic: teh-NEH-mohs THOO-mohs, KAHS, AH-gwah kohn GAHS
responses.local: Tenemos zumos, Kas, agua con gas…

responses.english: Shall I get you a grape juice?

phrases.intent_en: Ask if they have bar snacks
phrases.cultural_note: Many Spanish bars serve free tapas with each drink — a tradition especially strong in Granada, León, and Almería. Elsewhere you may pay for 'pinchos' or 'montaditos'.

responses.english: Yes, we have olives, crisps and ham.
responses.phonetic: SEE, teh-NEH-mohs ah-thay-TOO-nahs, pah-TAH-tahs ee hah-MOHN
responses.local: Sí, tenemos aceitunas, patatas y jamón.

responses.english: The tapa comes with the drink.
responses.phonetic: lah TAH-pah BYEH-neh kohn lah beh-BEE-dah
responses.local: La tapa viene con la bebida.

phrases.cultural_note: Use this to settle up your tab. The bartender will total everything. Rounding up or leaving small change as a tip is appreciated.

responses.phonetic: KLAH-roh, SOHN TREYN-tah ee DOS EH-oo-rohs
responses.local: Claro, son treinta y dos euros.

responses.phonetic: PAH-gahn kohn tar-HEH-tah oh ehn eh-fehk-TEE-boh

phrases.text_local: Una botella de vino, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: OO-nah boh-TEH-yah deh BEE-noh, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: Ordering a bottle is great value in Spanish bars. Ask for 'vino de la casa' for the most affordable option — often a well-chosen regional wine.

responses.english: Denominación de origen or house wine?
responses.phonetic: kohn deh-noh-mee-nah-THYOHN deh oh-REE-hehn oh deh lah KAH-sah
responses.local: ¿Con denominación de origen o de la casa?

phrases.cultural_note: 'Café solo' is a short black espresso. 'Café con leche' (half coffee half hot milk) is the classic Spanish morning coffee. 'Cortado' is espresso with a dash of milk.

phrases.text_local: Un café con leche, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: oon kah-FEH kohn LEH-cheh, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: The most popular morning coffee in Spain — equal parts espresso and hot milk. Usually served in a glass or large cup.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if there's a table free
phrases.cultural_note: In busy Spanish cafés it's fine to share a table with strangers — just ask 'permiso' before sitting.

responses.english: Yes, that one over there is free.
responses.phonetic: SEE, EH-sah deh ah-YEE ehs-TÁ LEE-breh
responses.local: Sí, esa de allí está libre.

responses.english: We're full, please wait a moment.

phrases.cultural_note: In cafés you can also say 'La cuenta' or simply catch the waiter's eye and mime signing. Pay at the counter in many smaller cafés.

phrases.text_local: Un croissant / una napolitana, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: oon KRWAH-sahn / OO-nah nah-poh-lee-TAH-nah, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: 'Napolitana de chocolate' (chocolate puff pastry) is a Spanish café staple. Also popular: 'churros con chocolate' for breakfast, especially in winter.
Most Spanish cafés and bars have free WiFi. Just ask at the bar and they'll usually write it on a napkin or point you to a sign. In tourist areas it may be on the menu.

Takeaway coffee is becoming more common in Spanish cities, especially with younger locals. Traditional bars may serve it in a small plastic cup. 'Para tomar aquí' means you'd like to sit and have it there instead.

responses.english: We only do it in a glass.

phrases.cultural_note: Sugar sachets and stirrers are standard on Spanish café tables. Sweetener ('edulcorante' or brand name 'sacarina') is widely available. Just ask if you can't see it on the table.

phrases.text_local: Un zumo de naranja natural, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: oon THOO-moh deh nah-RAHN-hah nah-too-RAHL, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: Spain is famous for its oranges, and a freshly squeezed orange juice ('zumo natural') is a breakfast staple. Most cafés squeeze it fresh in front of you. The word 'natural' is the key — without it you might get a carton.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for a glass of water
phrases.text_local: Un vaso de agua, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: oon BAH-soh deh AH-gwah, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: Tap water in Spain is generally safe to drink and free to ask for ('agua del grifo'). Cafés will usually bring a small glass alongside your coffee if you ask. Bottled water ('agua embotellada') costs extra.

responses.phonetic: KLAH-roh, kohn GAHS oh seen GAHS

phrases.text_local: Tengo una reserva a nombre de…
phrases.pronunciation: TEHN-goh OO-nah reh-SEHR-bah ah NOHM-breh deh
phrases.cultural_note: Have your passport and booking confirmation ready. Spanish hotels typically check in from 2–3pm.

responses.english: One moment, I'll look up your reservation.
responses.phonetic: oon moh-MEHN-toh, BOY ah boos-KAR soo reh-SEHR-bah
responses.local: Un momento, voy a buscar su reserva.

phrases.pronunciation: KWAHL ehs lah kohn-trah-SEH-nyah dehl WEE-fee
phrases.cultural_note: WiFi is standard in Spanish hotels. The password is often on a card in your room or at the front desk.

responses.english: It's on the card on the desk.
responses.phonetic: ehs-TÁ ehn lah tar-heh-TEE-tah SOH-breh ehl ehs-kree-TOH-ryoh
responses.local: Está en la tarjetita sobre el escritorio.

phrases.pronunciation: ah keh OH-rah ehs ehl deh-sah-YOO-noh
phrases.cultural_note: Breakfast in Spanish hotels is typically 7–10am. The 'desayuno' spread usually includes pan con tomate, jamón, pastries, and coffee.

responses.english: From seven to ten in the dining room.
responses.phonetic: deh SYEH-teh ah DYETH ehn ehl koh-meh-DOR
responses.local: De siete a diez en el comedor.

responses.english: Breakfast is included in your booking.
responses.phonetic: ehl deh-sah-YOO-noh ehs-TÁ een-kloo-EE-doh ehn soo reh-SEHR-bah
responses.local: El desayuno está incluido en su reserva.

phrases.pronunciation: ehs poh-SEE-bleh ah-THEHR oon CHEK-owt tar-DEE-oh
phrases.cultural_note: Standard checkout in Spain is noon. Late checkout is often possible for a fee or if the hotel has availability — always ask the evening before.

responses.english: Sorry, the hotel is fully booked.
responses.phonetic: loh see-EHN-toh, ehl oh-TEHL ehs-TÁ kohm-PLEH-toh
responses.local: Lo siento, el hotel está completo.

phrases.intent_en: Report a problem with the room
phrases.text_local: Hay un problema en la habitación.
phrases.pronunciation: AY oon proh-BLEH-mah ehn lah ah-bee-tah-THYOHN
phrases.cultural_note: Report issues promptly — Spanish hotel staff are generally responsive. For noise complaints, the front desk can often call the other room directly.

responses.english: What kind of problem is it?

phrases.text_local: ¿Pueden guardar el equipaje hasta más tarde?
phrases.pronunciation: PWEH-dehn gwar-DAR ehl eh-kee-PAH-heh AHS-tah MÁS TAR-deh
phrases.cultural_note: Most hotels offer free luggage storage after checkout. Ask at reception — they'll give you a tag for your bags.

responses.english: Of course, we'll give you a receipt.
responses.phonetic: por soo-PWEHS-toh, leh DAH-mohs oon rehs-GWAR-doh
responses.local: Por supuesto, le damos un resguardo.

responses.english: You can collect it whenever you like.

phrases.cultural_note: Wake-up calls are standard in Spanish hotels. Give a specific time — the front desk will call your room.

responses.english: Of course, we'll call you at seven.
responses.phonetic: por soo-PWEHS-toh, leh yah-MAH-mohs ah lahs SYEH-teh
responses.local: Por supuesto, le llamamos a las siete.

phrases.cultural_note: Many Spanish hotels skip daily cleaning unless requested. Use the room card or ask reception.

responses.english: Of course, we'll come in the afternoon.
The room will be ready at two.
responses.phonetic: lah ah-bee-tah-THYOHN ehs-tah-RAH LEES-tah ah lahs DOS
responses.local: La habitación estará lista a las dos.

phrases.text_local: Hay mucho ruido en la habitación de al lado.
phrases.pronunciation: AY MOO-choh RWEE-doh ehn lah ah-bee-tah-THYOHN deh ahl LAH-doh
phrases.cultural_note: Report noise to reception — they can call the neighbouring room. Spanish hotels typically observe quiet hours after 11pm.

responses.english: Would you like us to move you to another room?
responses.phonetic: KYEH-reh keh leh kahm-BYEH-mohs deh ah-bee-tah-THYOHN

phrases.text_local: La ducha no funciona. / No hay agua caliente.
phrases.pronunciation: lah DOO-chah NOH foonk-THYOH-nah. / NOH AY AH-gwah kah-LYEHN-teh
phrases.cultural_note: Report issues promptly — Spanish hotel maintenance responds quickly. If unfixable, request a room change.

responses.english: Shall we move you to another room?

phrases.text_local: Quiero hacer el check-out, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: KYEH-roh ah-THEHR ehl CHEK-owt, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: Standard checkout in Spain is noon. Hand in your key card at reception. Ask for a 'factura' if you need a receipt for expenses.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where the check-in desk is
phrases.pronunciation: DON-deh ehs-TÁ ehl mohs-trah-DOR deh fahk-too-rah-THYOHN
phrases.cultural_note: Spain's main airports: Madrid Barajas (MAD), Barcelona El Prat (BCN), Málaga (AGP), Palma (PMI). Check-in desks are usually numbered by airline.

responses.english: The desks are in terminal T4.
responses.phonetic: lohs mohs-trah-DOH-rehs ehs-TÁN ehn lah tehr-mee-NAHL teh-KWAH-troh
responses.local: Los mostradores están en la terminal T4.

responses.english: Follow the signs for your airline.
responses.phonetic: SEE-gah lohs kar-TEH-lehs deh soo ah-eh-roh-LEE-neh-ah
responses.local: Siga los carteles de su aerolínea.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where the departure gate is
phrases.pronunciation: DON-deh ehs-TÁ lah PWEHR-tah deh ehm-BAR-keh
phrases.cultural_note: Gates in Spanish airports are announced about 30–45 minutes before departure. Check the departures board ('salidas') regularly.

responses.english: Gate D42 is at the back on the right.
responses.phonetic: lah PWEHR-tah deh-KWAH-rehn-tah-ee-DOS ehs-TÁ ahl FOHN-doh ah lah deh-REH-chah
responses.local: La puerta D42 está al fondo a la derecha.

responses.english: No gate has been assigned yet.
responses.phonetic: toh-dah-BEE-ah NOH seh ah ah-seeg-NAH-doh PWEHR-tah
responses.local: Todavía no se ha asignado puerta.

phrases.pronunciation: DON-deh ehs lah reh-koh-HEE-dah deh eh-kee-PAH-hehs
phrases.cultural_note: Follow signs for 'Recogida de equipajes'. If your bag doesn't appear, report it immediately to the airline desk — don't leave the baggage area.

responses.english: It's on the ground floor, belt number three.
responses.phonetic: ehs ehn lah PLAHN-tah BAH-hah, THEEN-tah NOO-meh-roh TREHS
responses.local: Es en la planta baja, cinta número tres.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if the flight is on time
phrases.cultural_note: Key terms: 'en hora' (on time), 'retrasado' (delayed), 'cancelado' (cancelled), 'embarcando' (boarding).

responses.phonetic: ehl BWEH-loh SAH-leh ah soo OH-rah
responses.local: El vuelo sale a su hora.

responses.phonetic: AY oon reh-TRAH-soh deh kwah-REHN-tah mee-NOO-tohs
responses.local: Hay un retraso de cuarenta minutos.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where the taxi rank is
phrases.pronunciation: DON-deh ehs-TÁ lah pah-RAH-dah deh TAK-sees
phrases.cultural_note: Official airport taxis in Spain have regulated fares. In Madrid there's a flat rate from Barajas to the city centre. Avoid unofficial drivers inside the terminal.

responses.phonetic: ahl sah-LEER, HEE-reh ah lah deh-REH-chah
responses.local: Al salir, gire a la derecha.

responses.english: The taxi rank is on the ground floor.
responses.phonetic: lah pah-RAH-dah deh TAK-sees ehs ehn lah PLAHN-tah BAH-hah
responses.local: La parada de taxis es en la planta baja.

phrases.text_local: Un billete a Barcelona, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: oon bee-YEH-teh ah bar-theh-LOH-nah, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: Spain has excellent high-speed rail (AVE). Buy tickets in advance on Renfe.com — prices rise sharply close to departure. Validate regional train tickets before boarding.

phrases.pronunciation: ehn keh ahn-DEHN SAH-leh ehl TREHN
phrases.cultural_note: Platform numbers appear on the departures board ('salidas') about 15 minutes before departure for high-speed trains.

responses.phonetic: toh-dah-BEE-ah NOH seh SAH-beh ehl ahn-DEHN
responses.local: Todavía no se sabe el andén.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if the train is direct
phrases.text_local: ¿Este tren es directo o hay que hacer transbordo?
phrases.pronunciation: EHS-teh TREHN ehs dee-REHK-toh oh AY keh ah-THEHR trans-BOR-doh
phrases.cultural_note: 'Transbordo' means a change of train. AVE high-speed trains are usually direct between major cities. Regional trains often require changes.

responses.english: You need to change in Zaragoza.

phrases.cultural_note: Renfe AVE trains have a punctuality guarantee — if delayed more than 15 minutes, you may be entitled to a refund.

responses.phonetic: YEH-bah OO-nohs BEYN-teh mee-NOO-tohs deh reh-TRAH-soh
responses.local: Lleva unos veinte minutos de retraso.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where to validate the ticket
phrases.pronunciation: DON-deh AY keh bah-lee-DAR ehl bee-YEH-teh
phrases.cultural_note: AVE tickets don't need validation — just show them at the gate. Regional Cercanías and some local trains require validation at yellow machines on the platform.

responses.english: In the yellow machines on the platform.
responses.phonetic: ehn lahs MAH-kee-nahs ah-mah-REE-yahs dehl ahn-DEHN
responses.local: En las máquinas amarillas del andén.

phrases.text_local: Un billete de ida y vuelta a…, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: oon bee-YEH-teh deh EE-dah ee BWEHL-tah ah..., por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: Return tickets on Spanish AVE and long-distance trains can be significantly cheaper than two singles, especially booked in advance. Specify your return date and train preference. Renfe's 'tarifa flexible' allows changes; cheaper fares are non-refundable.

responses.english: For what date would you like to return?

phrases.intent_en: Ask if seat reservation is required
phrases.cultural_note: On Spanish AVE and long-distance trains (Alvia, Intercity), seat reservation is compulsory and included in the ticket price. On shorter regional trains ('cercanías'), it is not. Always clarify when buying your ticket.

responses.english: Yes, the seat is included in the ticket.
responses.phonetic: SEE, ehl ah-SYEHN-toh BYEH-neh een-kloo-EE-doh ehn ehl bee-YEH-teh
responses.local: Sí, el asiento viene incluido en el billete.

responses.english: No, you can sit wherever you like.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where the left-luggage lockers are
phrases.cultural_note: Automated luggage lockers ('consignas') are available at most major Spanish stations (Atocha, Barcelona Sants, Valencia). They charge by the hour and accept card payment. Staffed left-luggage offices are less common.

responses.english: The lockers are on the ground floor, near the exit.
responses.phonetic: lahs kohn-SEEG-nahs ehs-TAHN ehn lah PLAHN-tah BAH-hah, HOON-toh ah lah sah-LEE-dah
responses.local: Las consignas están en la planta baja, junto a la salida.

responses.phonetic: ehn EHS-tah ehs-tah-THYOHN NOH AY kohn-SEEG-nahs
responses.local: En esta estación no hay consignas.

phrases.intent_en: Ask about the next trains to your destination
phrases.pronunciation: KWAH-lehs SOHN lohs PROHK-see-mohs TREH-nehs ah
phrases.cultural_note: Real-time departure boards are displayed at all Spanish stations. Renfe's app gives live train times and allows seat selection. Staff at the information desk ('información') can print timetables and check availability.

responses.english: The next one leaves at quarter past twelve, platform five.
responses.phonetic: ehl PROHK-see-moh SAH-leh ah lahs DOH-theh ee KWAR-toh, ahn-DEHN THEEN-koh
responses.local: El próximo sale a las doce y cuarto, andén cinco.

phrases.intent_en: Tell the staff about a connection you need to make
phrases.text_local: Tengo un enlace en… ¿llegaré a tiempo?
phrases.pronunciation: TEHN-goh oon ehn-LAH-theh ehn... yeh-gah-REH ah TYEHM-poh
phrases.cultural_note: Spanish train staff can check connection times in real time. If your train is delayed and you miss a reserved connection, report immediately to the 'atención al cliente' desk. Renfe will usually rebook you on the next available service.

responses.english: Yes, you have plenty of time.
It's very tight. If there's a delay, tell the inspector.
responses.phonetic: ehs mooy HWOOS-toh. see AY reh-TRAH-soh, ah-BEE-seh ahl reh-bee-SOR
responses.local: Es muy justo. Si hay retraso, avise al revisor.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where the nearest metro station is
phrases.text_local: ¿Dónde está la estación de metro más cercana?
phrases.pronunciation: DON-deh ehs-TÁ lah ehs-tah-THYOHN deh MEH-troh MÁS thehr-KAH-nah
phrases.cultural_note: Madrid and Barcelona have excellent metro networks. Madrid Metro is open until 1:30am. Look for the red 'M' sign (Madrid) or red diamond (Barcelona).

responses.english: There's a metro entrance right across.
responses.phonetic: AY OO-nah BOH-kah deh MEH-troh ah-KEE ehn-FREHN-teh
responses.local: Hay una boca de metro aquí enfrente.

phrases.intent_en: Ask how to buy a ticket
phrases.cultural_note: Buy a 10-trip 'Metrobús' card for best value — it works on metro and bus. Single tickets cost more. Machines have English language option.

responses.english: At the machines, they take card.
responses.phonetic: ehn lahs MAH-kee-nahs ow-toh-MAH-tee-kahs, ahk-THEHP-tahn tar-HEH-tah
responses.local: En las máquinas automáticas, aceptan tarjeta.

responses.english: Do you want a single or a 10-trip card?
responses.phonetic: KYEH-reh oon bee-YEH-teh sehn-THEE-yoh oh OO-nah tar-HEH-tah deh DYETH BYAH-hehs
responses.local: ¿Quiere un billete sencillo o una tarjeta de diez viajes?

phrases.cultural_note: Metro lines in Spain are numbered and colour-coded. Show the station name on your phone if you're not sure how to pronounce it.

responses.phonetic: KOH-heh lah LEE-neh-ah DOS dee-rehk-THYOHN KWAH-troh kah-MEE-nohs
responses.local: Coge la línea dos dirección Cuatro Caminos.

responses.english: You need to change at Sol.
responses.phonetic: AY keh ah-THEHR trans-BOR-doh ehn SOHL
responses.local: Hay que hacer transbordo en Sol.

phrases.cultural_note: Metro maps are available at every station. Download the city's official metro app for live journey planning.

phrases.intent_en: Ask when the last train is
phrases.text_local: ¿A qué hora es el último metro?
phrases.pronunciation: ah keh OH-rah ehs ehl OOL-tee-moh MEH-troh
phrases.cultural_note: Madrid Metro closes around 1:30am on weekdays, 2:30am on weekends. Barcelona Metro runs 24 hours on Fridays and Saturdays.

responses.phonetic: ehl OOL-tee-moh ehs lah OO-nah ee MEH-dyah
responses.local: El último es la una y media.

responses.english: On Fridays the metro doesn't close.
responses.phonetic: lohs BYEHR-nehs ehl MEH-troh NOH THYEH-rrah
responses.local: Los viernes el metro no cierra.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where to transfer to another line
phrases.text_local: ¿Dónde hago el transbordo para la línea…?
phrases.pronunciation: DON-deh AH-goh ehl trans-BOR-doh PAH-rah lah LEE-neh-ah
phrases.cultural_note: Madrid has one of Europe's largest metro networks with 13 lines. Most major interchanges are clearly signed with the line number and colour. Sol, Nuevos Ministerios, and Atocha are the main interchange hubs.

phrases.intent_en: Ask about a tourist pass or multi-trip card
phrases.text_local: ¿Hay algún abono turístico o bono de transporte?
phrases.pronunciation: AY ahl-GOON ah-BOH-noh too-REES-tee-koh oh BOH-noh deh trans-POR-teh
phrases.cultural_note: Madrid's 'Tourist Travel Pass' (Abono Turístico) covers 1–7 days on all metro, bus, and commuter rail within a chosen zone. Available at airport metro machines and main stations. Great value for a multi-day visit.

responses.english: Yes, there's a tourist pass for one to seven days.
responses.phonetic: SEE, AY oon PAH-seh too-REES-tee-koh deh OO-noh ah SYEH-teh DEE-ahs
responses.local: Sí, hay un pase turístico de uno a siete días.

responses.english: You can buy it at the machines at the entrance.
responses.phonetic: PWEH-deh kohm-PRAR-loh ehn lahs MAH-kee-nahs deh lah ehn-TRAH-dah
responses.local: Puede comprarlo en las máquinas de la entrada.

phrases.intent_en: Ask how many stops to your destination
phrases.cultural_note: Counting stops is the most reliable way to navigate, especially with unfamiliar station names. Show the stop name on your phone screen if needed — other passengers or station staff are nearly always happy to help.

responses.english: You need to transfer at Nuevos Ministerios.
responses.phonetic: TYEH-neh keh ah-THEHR trans-BOR-doh ehn NWEH-bohs mee-nees-TEH-ryohs
responses.local: Tiene que hacer transbordo en Nuevos Ministerios.

phrases.intent_en: Ask which exit to take for a street or landmark
phrases.cultural_note: Madrid metro stations are large and can have many exits. The exits are labelled with street names or landmarks — taking the right one can save you several minutes of reorientation on the surface.

responses.phonetic: lah sah-LEE-dah TREHS ehs lah mahs thehr-KAH-nah
responses.local: La salida 3 es la más cercana.

responses.english: Follow the signs for the Prado Museum.
responses.phonetic: SEE-gah lohs kar-TEH-les dehl moo-SEH-oh dehl PRAH-doh
responses.local: Siga los carteles del Museo del Prado.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if the station has an elevator / lift
phrases.cultural_note: Accessibility in Spanish metro stations varies. Madrid has been upgrading lifts ('ascensores') across the network but some older stations still lack them. The Metro de Madrid app shows which stations are step-free.

responses.english: Yes, it's at the end of the platform.
responses.phonetic: SEE, ehs-TÁ ahl fee-NAHL dehl ahn-DEHN
responses.local: Sí, está al final del andén.

responses.english: No, there are only stairs in this station.
responses.phonetic: NOH, SOH-loh AY es-kah-LEH-rahs ehn EHS-tah ehs-tah-THYOHN
responses.local: No, solo hay escaleras en esta estación.

phrases.cultural_note: Spanish taxis are metered and regulated. Show the address on your phone if unsure of pronunciation. Night rates ('tarifa nocturna') apply after 9pm.

phrases.text_local: ¿Cuánto cuesta más o menos ir a…?
phrases.pronunciation: KWAHN-toh KWEHS-tah MÁS oh MEH-nohs eer ah
phrases.cultural_note: Taxis in Spain are metered — drivers can give an estimate but the meter is the official fare. Airport flat rates exist in some cities (e.g., Madrid: €30 fixed to/from Barajas).

responses.english: About twelve euros, depends on traffic.
responses.phonetic: OO-nohs DOH-theh EH-oo-rohs, deh-PEHN-deh dehl TRÁH-fee-koh
responses.local: Unos doce euros, depende del tráfico.

responses.english: There's a fixed rate to the airport.

phrases.cultural_note: Taxis in Spain are required to provide receipts on request. Useful for expense claims — ask before paying.

responses.phonetic: lah MAH-kee-nah ehs-TÁ ROH-tah, loh see-EHN-toh
responses.local: La máquina está rota, lo siento.

phrases.cultural_note: Say this clearly when approaching your stop. The driver will pull over safely.

phrases.intent_en: Ask the driver to turn on the meter
phrases.cultural_note: All licensed Spanish taxis must use the meter. If you're going to/from an airport, there may be a fixed fare — ask first. In Madrid and Barcelona, a minimum fare applies regardless of distance.

responses.english: To the airport there's a fixed fare.

phrases.intent_en: Ask to take the fastest route
phrases.text_local: Vaya por la ruta más rápida, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: BAH-yah por lah ROO-tah mahs RAH-pee-dah, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: Spanish drivers respond well to this. Most use navigation apps and will know alternate routes. In Madrid, the M-30 ring road can be fastest at certain times; in Barcelona, asking to avoid the Ramblas area helps.

responses.english: There's quite a bit of traffic, we'll take a little longer.
responses.local: Hay bastante tráfico, tardaremos un poco más.

phrases.cultural_note: AC is standard in Spanish licensed taxis. In summer (especially in Seville or Madrid where temperatures regularly exceed 40°C) this is a very normal request. You can also ask to open the window — '¿puede bajar la ventanilla?'

phrases.intent_en: Say you need to be somewhere by a specific time
phrases.text_local: Necesito estar allí a las… ¿llegamos a tiempo?
phrases.pronunciation: neh-theh-SEE-toh ehs-TAR ah-YEE ah lahs... yeh-GAH-mohs ah TYEHM-poh
phrases.cultural_note: Spanish drivers appreciate honesty about time pressure and will tell you straight whether it's realistic. State the time clearly and show your watch or phone screen if needed. 'Tengo prisa' (I'm in a hurry) reinforces the urgency.

responses.english: It'll be tough with this traffic.
responses.phonetic: bah ah sehr dee-FEE-theel kohn EHS-teh TRÁH-fee-koh
responses.local: Va a ser difícil con este tráfico.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if card payment is accepted
phrases.cultural_note: Card payment is widely accepted in Spanish taxis but it's always worth confirming before you start the journey. Most taxis in Madrid and Barcelona have card readers. In smaller cities, cash may be preferred.

phrases.text_local: Me duele la cabeza. ¿Qué me recomienda?
phrases.pronunciation: meh DWEH-leh lah kah-BEH-thah. keh meh reh-koh-MYEHN-dah
phrases.cultural_note: Pharmacists in Spain are highly trained and can dispense advice and many medications without a prescription. The green cross sign marks all pharmacies.

responses.english: Are you allergic to any medication?

phrases.intent_en: Ask for something for a stomach ache
phrases.text_local: Necesito algo para el dolor de estómago.
phrases.pronunciation: neh-theh-SEE-toh AHL-goh PAH-rah ehl doh-LOR deh ehs-TOH-mah-goh
phrases.cultural_note: Common options: 'Almax' (antacid), 'Fortasec' (for diarrhoea), 'Espasmofor' (for cramps). Pharmacists will ask about your symptoms before recommending.

responses.english: Is it heartburn, diarrhoea, or nausea?
Take this three times a day.
responses.phonetic: TOH-meh EHS-toh TREHS BEH-thehs ahl DEE-ah
responses.local: Tome esto tres veces al día.

phrases.cultural_note: Spain's sun is strong — factor 50 is recommended, especially in Andalusia and the Canary Islands in summer. Pharmacies stock a wide range of sun protection.

responses.phonetic: PAH-rah KAH-rah oh PAH-rah ehl KWEHR-poh

phrases.intent_en: Ask if you need a prescription
phrases.cultural_note: Many medicines available only on prescription in other countries are over-the-counter in Spain. Pharmacists can advise on what's available without a prescription.

responses.english: Yes, you need a prescription for that.
responses.phonetic: SEE, PAH-rah EH-soh neh-theh-SEE-tah reh-THEH-tah MEH-dee-kah
responses.local: Sí, para eso necesita receta médica.

responses.english: No, it's sold without a prescription.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for something for a cold
phrases.cultural_note: Common cold remedies in Spain: 'Frenadol' (multi-symptom), 'Couldina', 'Gelocatil' (paracetamol). Pharmacists will ask about specific symptoms.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for something for a fever
phrases.cultural_note: Paracetamol (Paracetamol, Gelocatil, Apiretal) is the standard first choice in Spain. Ibuprofen (Nurofen, Espidifen) is also widely available without a prescription.

responses.english: I recommend paracetamol every eight hours.
responses.phonetic: leh reh-koh-MYEHN-doh pah-rah-seh-tah-MOHL KAH-dah OH-choh OH-rahs
responses.local: Le recomiendo paracetamol cada ocho horas.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for something for diarrhoea or nausea
phrases.pronunciation: TEHN-goh dyah-RREH-ah / NAH-oo-seh-ahs. keh meh reh-koh-MYEHN-dah
phrases.cultural_note: For diarrhoea: Fortasec (loperamide) or Smecta. For nausea: Primperan. Stay hydrated — 'suero oral' (oral rehydration salts) are available at all pharmacies.

responses.english: I'll give you Fortasec and rehydration salts.
responses.phonetic: leh DOY for-tah-SEK ee AH-gwah deh SAH-lehs
responses.local: Le doy Fortasec y agua de sales.

responses.english: How long have you had these symptoms?

phrases.cultural_note: Spanish pharmacies stock full wound care. Common items: Betadine or Clorhexidina (antiseptic), tiritas (plasters), gasas (gauze), vendas (bandages).

phrases.pronunciation: TEHN-goh OO-nah reh-ak-THYOHN ah-LEHR-hee-kah. TYEH-nehn ahn-tee-ees-tah-MEE-nee-koh
phrases.cultural_note: Common antihistamines in Spain: Zyrtec (cetirizina), Loratadina, Aerius. Available without prescription. Pharmacists advise on non-drowsy ('no somnoliento') options.

responses.english: I recommend this antihistamine, one a day.
responses.phonetic: leh reh-koh-MYEHN-doh EHS-teh ahn-tee-ees-tah-MEE-nee-koh, OO-noh ahl DEE-ah
responses.local: Le recomiendo este antihistamínico, uno al día.

phrases.intent_en: Ask how to take a medicine
phrases.cultural_note: Spanish pharmacists always explain dosage. Key words: 'comprimido' (tablet), 'cápsula' (capsule), 'veces al día' (times per day), 'con el estómago lleno' (with food), 'con agua' (with water).

responses.english: One tablet three times a day, with water.
responses.phonetic: oon kohm-pree-MEE-doh TREHS BEH-thehs ahl DEE-ah, kohn AH-gwah
responses.local: Un comprimido tres veces al día, con agua.

phrases.cultural_note: Spain's top museums: Prado and Reina Sofía (Madrid), Guggenheim (Bilbao), MACBA (Barcelona). Many offer free entry in the last 2 hours of the day — check before buying.
Many Spanish museums allow photos without flash. Some temporary exhibitions prohibit it. Always check signs or ask — rules vary room by room.
Most Spanish museums require you to leave large bags at the cloakroom ('guardarropa'). It's usually free.

responses.english: At the cloakroom, on your right.
responses.phonetic: ehn ehl gwar-dah-RROH-pah, ah soo deh-REH-chah
responses.local: En el guardarropa, a su derecha.

responses.english: It's free and it's at the entrance.
responses.phonetic: ehs grah-TWEE-toh ee ehs-TÁ ehn lah ehn-TRAH-dah
responses.local: Es gratuito y está en la entrada.

phrases.cultural_note: Audio guides are available in most major Spanish museums. Many now offer app-based guides you can download on your phone for free — ask at reception.

responses.english: Yes, the audio guide costs five euros.
responses.phonetic: SEE, lah ow-dyoh-GEE-ah KWEHS-tah THEEN-koh EH-oo-rohs
responses.local: Sí, la audioguía cuesta cinco euros.

responses.english: You can download the app for free.

phrases.pronunciation: KWAHL ehs ehl oh-RAH-ryoh deh ah-pehr-TOO-rah
phrases.cultural_note: Many Spanish museums close on Mondays. The Prado and Reina Sofía are open Tuesday–Sunday. Most offer free or reduced entry in the last two hours of the day.
Spanish museums offer discounts for students, seniors, and the unemployed. EU citizens under 18 often enter free. Many offer free entry on certain days — check before buying.

responses.english: Yes, with a student card it's half price.
responses.phonetic: SEE, kohn kar-NEH deh ehs-too-DYAHN-teh ehs lah mee-TAHD
responses.local: Sí, con carné de estudiante es la mitad.

responses.phonetic: lohs doh-MEEN-gohs lah ehn-TRAH-dah ehs grah-TWEE-tah
responses.local: Los domingos la entrada es gratuita.

phrases.cultural_note: Guided tours at major Spanish museums (Prado, Guggenheim) often sell out — book online. English tours are common in big cities, less so in smaller regional museums.

responses.english: Yes, the next one is at four o'clock.
responses.phonetic: SEE, lah PROHK-see-mah ehs ah lahs KWAH-troh
responses.local: Sí, la próxima es a las cuatro.

responses.english: English guided tours are at eleven.
responses.phonetic: lahs bee-SEE-tahs ghee-AH-dahs ehn een-GLÉS SOHN ah lahs OHN-theh
responses.local: Las visitas guiadas en inglés son a las once.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where a specific exhibit is
phrases.cultural_note: Show the name on your phone or brochure if pronunciation is tricky. Free maps are usually available at the ticket desk.

responses.english: It's on the second floor, right wing.
responses.phonetic: ehs-TÁ ehn lah seh-GOON-dah PLAHN-tah, AH-lah deh-REH-chah
responses.local: Está en la segunda planta, ala derecha.

responses.phonetic: ah-KEE TYEH-neh ehl PLAH-noh dehl moo-SEH-oh
responses.local: Aquí tiene el plano del museo.

phrases.text_local: ¿El museo es accesible para sillas de ruedas?
phrases.pronunciation: ehl moo-SEH-oh ehs ak-theh-SEE-bleh PAH-rah SEE-yahs deh RWEH-dahs
phrases.cultural_note: Spanish museums are required to provide accessibility. Lifts and ramps are standard in major museums. Wheelchairs are often available to borrow free of charge at the entrance.

responses.english: Yes, there are lifts and ramps on all floors.
responses.phonetic: SEE, AY ah-thehn-SOR ee RRAHM-pahs ehn TOH-dahs lahs PLAHN-tahs
responses.local: Sí, hay ascensor y rampas en todas las plantas.

responses.english: You can request a wheelchair at reception.
responses.phonetic: PWEH-deh soh-lee-thee-TAR OO-nah SEE-yah deh RWEH-dahs ehn reh-thehp-THYOHN
responses.local: Puede solicitar una silla de ruedas en recepción.

phrases.pronunciation: dees-KOOL-peh, DON-deh ehs-TÁ lah sehk-THYOHN deh
phrases.cultural_note: Major Spanish supermarket chains: Mercadona, Carrefour, El Corte Inglés, Lidl, Día. Staff are generally helpful with directions inside the store.

responses.english: At the back on the left.

phrases.pronunciation: meh dah OO-nah BOHL-sah, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: Plastic bags cost a small fee in Spain (usually 5–25 cents) under anti-plastic legislation. Bring a reusable bag or ask for a paper one ('bolsa de papel').

phrases.intent_en: Ask if they accept card payment
phrases.cultural_note: Card payment is widely accepted in Spanish supermarkets. Contactless is standard. Some smaller local shops may still prefer cash.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where the fresh produce is
phrases.text_local: ¿Dónde está la fruta y la verdura?
phrases.pronunciation: DON-deh ehs-TÁ lah FROO-tah ee lah behr-DOO-rah
phrases.cultural_note: Spanish supermarkets have excellent fresh produce sections. Markets ('mercados') are even better — try Mercado de la Boqueria (Barcelona) or Mercado de San Miguel (Madrid).

responses.english: When you enter, on the left.
It's at the back of the supermarket.

phrases.cultural_note: Spanish supermarkets ('supermercados') typically have staffed tills ('cajas') and an increasing number of self-service checkouts ('cajas automáticas'). Express lanes for few items are common in larger stores.

responses.english: The checkout is at the back on the right.
responses.phonetic: lah KAH-hah ehs-TÁ ahl FOHN-doh ah lah deh-REH-chah
responses.local: La caja está al fondo a la derecha.

responses.english: The self-checkouts are near the entrance.
responses.phonetic: lahs KAH-hahs ow-toh-MAH-tee-kahs ehs-TAHN ah lah ehn-TRAH-dah
responses.local: Las cajas automáticas están a la entrada.

phrases.intent_en: Say you don't need a bag
phrases.cultural_note: Single-use plastic bags are charged for in Spain (around 5–10 cents). Most shoppers bring their own reusable bag ('bolsa reutilizable') or buy one at the till. Saying this is normal and appreciated.

responses.english: Do you have your own bag?

phrases.cultural_note: If a price tag is missing, ask a member of staff ('empleado/a') or take the item to the customer service desk. In Spanish supermarkets, the till price always overrides the shelf label if there's a discrepancy — knowing this is useful.

phrases.intent_en: Ask about returning or exchanging something
phrases.cultural_note: Returns ('devoluciones') in Spain require a receipt ('ticket'). Large chains like Mercadona and El Corte Inglés have clear return policies. Go to the customer service desk ('atención al cliente') near the entrance.

responses.english: Please go to the customer service desk.

phrases.intent_en: Ask where the organic or fresh produce section is
phrases.text_local: ¿Dónde está la sección de productos ecológicos?
phrases.pronunciation: DON-deh ehs-TÁ lah sehk-THYOHN deh proh-DOOK-tohs eh-koh-LOH-hee-kohs
phrases.cultural_note: Organic products ('ecológicos') are clearly labelled in Spanish supermarkets. Mercadona, Eroski, and Carrefour all have dedicated organic sections. The EU organic logo (green leaf) appears on all certified products.

responses.english: The organic products are in aisle four.
responses.phonetic: lohs proh-DOOK-tohs eh-koh-LOH-hee-kohs ehs-TAHN ehn ehl pah-SEE-yoh KWAH-troh
responses.local: Los productos ecológicos están en el pasillo cuatro.

responses.english: There's an organic section at the back.
responses.phonetic: AY OO-nah sehk-THYOHN BEE-oh ahl FOHN-doh
responses.local: Hay una sección bio al fondo.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for a loaf of bread
phrases.text_local: Una barra de pan, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: OO-nah BAH-rrah deh PAHN, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: 'Una barra' is the classic Spanish white bread loaf, similar to a baguette. Also popular: 'pan de pueblo' (rustic round loaf), 'pan de centeno' (rye bread).

responses.english: Do you want it whole or half?
responses.phonetic: lah KYEH-reh ehn-TEH-rah oh ah mee-TAHD

responses.english: Here you go, fresh from the oven.
responses.phonetic: ah-KEE TYEH-neh, reh-THYEHN sah-LEE-dah dehl OR-noh
responses.local: Aquí tiene, recién salida del horno.

phrases.cultural_note: Spanish bakeries ('panaderías') are also pastry shops. Look for: 'ensaimada' (Mallorca), 'polvorón' (Andalusia), 'xuixo' (Catalonia), 'croissant de mantequilla'.

responses.english: We have croissants, chocolate pastries, and palmiers.
The churros run out in half an hour.
responses.phonetic: lohs CHOO-rrohs seh ah-KAH-bahn ehn MEH-dyah OH-rah
responses.local: Los churros se acaban en media hora.

phrases.cultural_note: Bread is very affordable in Spain — a barra costs around 60–90 cents. Artisan bakeries charge more but quality is noticeably higher.

responses.phonetic: TOH-doh HOON-toh SOHN DOS kohn BEYN-teh
responses.local: Todo junto son dos con veinte.

phrases.intent_en: Ask if they accept card payment
phrases.cultural_note: Smaller bakeries in Spain often prefer cash, especially for small purchases. Many now have card terminals but it's worth asking.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for the bread to be sliced
phrases.pronunciation: meh loh PWEH-deh kor-TAR ehn reh-bah-NAH-dahs
phrases.cultural_note: Many Spanish bakeries have a bread slicer. If you're buying for a picnic or sandwich, ask — it's a common and easy request.

Point at the item and hold up two fingers — this combination works in any Spanish panadería. Staff are very used to pointing-and-smiling navigation. 'De esas' is the feminine form if the item (like 'napolitana') is feminine.

responses.english: Here you go, one euro eighty.
responses.phonetic: ah-KEE TYEH-neh, oon EH-oo-roh kohn oh-CHEHN-tah
responses.local: Aquí tiene, un euro con ochenta.

phrases.cultural_note: Spanish panaderías typically bake bread in the morning and early afternoon. This question often results in a warm recommendation — the staff are proud of what's just come out. In the afternoon, ask specifically about 'pan del día' (bread of the day).

responses.english: The country loaf just came out of the oven.
responses.phonetic: lah oh-GAH-thah ah-KAH-bah deh sah-LEER dehl OR-noh
responses.local: La hogaza acaba de salir del horno.

phrases.text_local: Un bocadillo de jamón, por favor.
phrases.pronunciation: oon boh-kah-DEE-yoh deh hah-MOHN, por fah-BOR
phrases.cultural_note: The bocadillo (bread roll sandwich) is a Spanish staple, not just for panaderías. Jamón serrano is the most common filling; in quality delis, jamón ibérico is available for more. You can also try 'queso' (cheese), 'tortilla' (Spanish omelette), or 'lomo' (pork loin).

phrases.intent_en: Ask if there are gluten-free options
phrases.cultural_note: Gluten-free ('sin gluten') awareness has grown significantly in Spain. Specialist gluten-free bakeries and sections in larger supermarkets are now common in cities. Show this phrase on your phone if you're unsure — cross-contamination can be a concern in traditional bakeries.

responses.english: Yes, we have gluten-free bread and pastries.
responses.phonetic: SEE, teh-NEH-mohs PAHN ee boh-yeh-REE-ah seen GLOO-tehn
responses.local: Sí, tenemos pan y bollería sin gluten.

responses.english: I'm sorry, we don't have gluten-free products.
responses.phonetic: loh see-EHN-toh, NOH teh-NEH-mohs proh-DOOK-tohs seen GLOO-tehn
responses.local: Lo siento, no tenemos productos sin gluten.
